meta.titulo_pagina: A1 Guide: Describing People in Spanish
meta.objetivo: Identify physical traits, use adjectives, and confirm information.

fichas.explicacion: These expressions help you get someone's attention using their name in a very natural way.

items: Excuse me, Juan, do you have the time?

fichas.consejo: Use the person's name to be more polite.

fichas.explicacion: Use these simple phrases to introduce a new person to your friends or coworkers.

items: Let me introduce you to my friend Lucía.
Let me introduce you to my teacher.

fichas.consejo: Use "te presento" in situations with friends and family.

fichas.explicacion: These are very important polite words you should say right after meeting someone for the first time.
fichas.consejo: “Encantado” is for men and “Encantada” for women.

fichas.explicacion: We use these small words before nouns to talk about things or people we already know very well.

items: The house of my friend is big.

fichas.consejo: The article must match the noun in gender and number.

fichas.explicacion: These words are used to introduce new information or to talk about one thing among many in a general way.

items: I have some books at my house.
There are some photos on the table.

fichas.consejo: Use "un" for masculine and "una" for feminine singular.

fichas.explicacion: In Spanish, the gender and number of words must always be the same within the same sentence.
fichas.consejo: If the noun is feminine, the adjective also ends in -a.

fichas.explicacion: We use this verb form to indicate that an object or a person is currently present in a specific place.

items: There is a photo of my friend here.
There are ten students in my class.
There is a pen on the table.

fichas.consejo: The word "hay" never changes; it's always written the same way.

fichas.explicacion: Use these words after the verb 'to be' to explain which country or place in the world you are from.
fichas.consejo: Nationalities in Spanish are written with a lowercase letter.

fichas.explicacion: It's essential to separate groups of words with short pauses so that others can understand your message better.

items: No, Elena. [pause] She's of medium height.
My friend is tall, [pause] blonde, and nice.

fichas.consejo: Take a quick breath at every comma to sound more natural.

fichas.explicacion: When you state something with confidence, your tone of voice should drop a bit as you reach the end of the sentence.
fichas.consejo: Lower your pitch at the end to show you've finished speaking.

fichas.explicacion: This punctuation mark is necessary to separate sentences and mark the end of a complete idea within a text.

items: My cat is black. I am tall.

fichas.consejo: Remember to always use a capital letter after a period.

fichas.explicacion: We use gentle forms and indirect descriptions to avoid being too direct or coming across as a bit rude when talking to others.
fichas.consejo: Use "un poco" to soften adjectives that seem negative.

fichas.explicacion: To create an emotional connection, we should focus our communication on people and their closest human relationships.
fichas.consejo: Mentioning people helps to have more interesting conversations.
